title: Black Coffee on Wool
stainChemistry: Black coffee tannins bond to wool through hydrogen attraction. Wool's delicate nature requires cold treatment (no heat triggers felting), gentle acid (not strong), and no aggressive chemistry. Acetic acid works on tannins but must be diluted to avoid weakening wool's fiber structure. Wool's own protein makes it sensitive to extended acid or oxidant exposure.
whyThisWorks: Cold water flushes tannins without felting risk. Diluted acetic acid breaks remaining bonds without weakening wool. Dilute peroxide oxidizes color without fiber damage. This approach removes coffee tannins while protecting wool's delicate protein structure.

spottingProtocol.instruction: Flush cold water from back. Blot from front. Never rub — wool felts with agitation. Work from stain edge inward.
Apply diluted acetic acid (1:1 with cold water). Work gently with sponge. Blot and check. Repeat if color remains. Never use full-strength acid on wool.
If brown color remains after acid step: apply dilute peroxide (3% diluted 1:1 with cold water). Sponge gently, blot, check. Keep brief — extended oxidizer contact weakens wool.
Flush thoroughly to remove all acid and peroxide residue. Work from back to front. Water must run completely clear.

aftercare: Squeeze gently in towel — never wring. Lay flat on clean towel. Never use heat. If stain persists, professional dry cleaner with wool expertise is the next step.

homeSolutions.agent: Wool + black coffee: cold, gentle, one round
homeSolutions.instruction: Coffee is tannin; wool is protein fiber. Both ban alkaline (baking soda, ammonia, OxiClean) and both ban heat. Wool adds: no enzymes, no scrubbing, no wringing. Fresh black coffee on wool usually lifts with cold water alone — a lot of the stain sits on the fiber surface before it soaks in. Caught fast, this is one of the easier delicate-fabric wins.

Dab a drop of cold water on an inside seam. Wait 60 seconds, blot with a white cloth. If the seam loses color, skip to step 6. If clean: press a folded white cloth straight down on the stain. Lift, move to a dry section, press again. Work outside-in. Keep going until the cloth stops picking up brown. Do not wipe, do not rub.

homeSolutions.agent: Cold water back-flush — don't soak wool
homeSolutions.instruction: Turn the garment inside out. Run COLD water through the BACK of the stain for 30–60 seconds. Don't plunge or submerge the whole area — wool absorbs water fast and the wetter it gets, the higher the felt risk under any friction. Short targeted flush, then blot between dry towels.

If a shadow remains, mix 1 part white vinegar with 1 part cold water. Press a cotton swab onto the spot. 60 SECONDS MAX on wool — set a timer. Rinse with cold water from the back. The acid keeps tannin in its lighter form and restores wool's natural pH (~5). Over-dwell weakens wool fiber, so the timer is load-bearing.
homeSolutions.safetyNote: Never use full-strength vinegar on wool. Never combine with bleach or peroxide on the same pass.

homeSolutions.agent: 3% peroxide dab — STRICT 60-second ceiling, light wool only
homeSolutions.instruction: If a BROWN shadow persists (not tan — tan lifts with another vinegar pass) AND the wool is white, cream, or very light AND the patch test came back clean: mix 1 part 3% peroxide with 1 part cold water. Dab with a cotton swab. 60 SECONDS MAXIMUM, flush with cold water immediately. Skip entirely on colored or dark wool — peroxide yellows wool at longer dwells or lifts darker dyes.
homeSolutions.safetyNote: Never exceed 60 seconds peroxide dwell on wool.

homeSolutions.agent: Lay flat to air-dry, reshape while damp
homeSolutions.instruction: Press between dry white towels to lift moisture — stack, press, don't wring. Lay flat on a fresh dry towel, reshape while damp (wool holds the shape it dries in). Air-dry at room temperature, out of direct sun. NEVER hang wet wool. NEVER apply heat. Don't run a second home round if a shadow persists — one round only on wool.

homeSolutions.agent: Escalate — wool specialist for anything that survives one round
homeSolutions.instruction: If the stain isn't gone after one full protocol, take the garment to a cleaner who specializes in wool (ask first — not every dry cleaner is wool-safe). Tell them: "Black coffee on wool, age [x]. Treated at home with cold water + 1:1 vinegar + [peroxide on white] — one round, strict 60-sec dwells. No hot water, no enzymes, no alkaline." Pros have sodium hydrosulfite and wool-safe tannin spotters for residual color.

materialWarnings: NEVER use bleach—damages wool fiber and dye
NEVER use aggressive oxidizers—can damage protein fiber
escalation: If coffee stain on wool persists after dilute acid + dilute peroxide treatment, or has browned/aged, call professional wool specialist. Professional may use wool-safe tannin spotters and access to appropriate reducing/oxidizing systems.

household.name: White Vinegar
household.note: For tannin removal. DILUTE 1:1 with cold water. Use 1 minute max. DO NOT substitute industrial-strength (28%) acetic acid — it causes chemical burns.

household.name: H₂O₂ (3%)
household.note: For tannin color. DILUTE 1:1 with cold water (makes ~1.5%). Wool is sensitive.

professional.name: StreeTAN
professional.note: Professional acetic acid spotter. Stronger than household vinegar, appropriate for professional shops.

customerExplanation: Black coffee tannins bond to wool. Cold water flushes the bulk of the stain. Diluted acid breaks remaining bonds. Diluted peroxide oxidizes residual color. Wool is delicate—we must use diluted chemistry and never heat, which causes permanent felting.
commonMistakes: Using hot water—this causes felting AND sets tannins deeper
Using full-strength acid—weakens wool's already-delicate protein structure
Not diluting peroxide—full-strength can damage wool
scienceNote: Tannin-wool bonding happens through hydrogen attraction and weak molecular grip forces. Wool's keratin protein has amino acids with polar groups (carboxylic acid -COOH, hydroxyl -OH) that attract tannins' hydroxyl groups. Lowering pH (acid) protonates some of these groups, weakening bonds. However, prolonged acid exposure can denature wool's own protein structure, so dilution and brief dwell times are critical. This is the opposite of what you might think—you'd expect strong acid to work better, but with wool, dilute + brief is the safe approach.
callPro: If black coffee stain on wool persists after home treatment with dilute acid and dilute peroxide, call professional. Risk of fiber damage with stronger chemistry is real.
solventNote: Professional: Ice water flush, then diluted acetic acid (GAA 1:10 in cold water, 1-2 min max). For brown/aged tannins, dilute H₂O₂ (1.5-2%, cold, 2 min max). For very stubborn brown stains, reducing bleach (StreePene sodium hydrosulfite 0.25-0.5%, cold, max 5 min) to reverse oxidation, then oxidize again. Temperature never exceeds 30°C. Test hidden area first. VDS post-treatment optional.
